comment.base:
This is unfortunate. You can hear her tell them to go around her car.
They tell her to get out of the car and pull on her door. She made the mistake of trying to drive off instead of obeying the agent's command. There happens to be another agent in front of her car (whom she hits) when she accelerates and turns her wheel to flee. The agent in front only has seconds to react, not knowing whether or not she was going to run him over. A life gone in a a split second.

comment.base:
God bless her soul. Moments like this are heartbreaking for everyone involved.
‎
A lot of encounters escalate simply because people misunderstand how to safely exercise their rights in the moment. The safest approach is almost always to stay calm, follow instructions, and use legal channels afterward if something was wrong. That principle protects citizens and officers alike, and it’s something we should teach clearly across all communities.
‎
Cultural Relationship With Law Enforcement (It Is Not Organic)
‎
Historically, people formed their perceptions of law enforcement through movies, music, books, and neighborhood stories. Those influences were mostly entertainment — not instruction.
But in recent years, institutional and political rhetoric has added new layers to that conditioning. These newer influences aren’t entertainment; they function more like guidance or mental framing. They shape expectations, fears, and assumptions long before any real‑world encounter happens.
For many people, their relationship with law enforcement isn’t based on personal experience at all — it’s shaped by the narratives and institutions that surround them.

comment.base:
If someone inside ICE wants to leave but stays because they’re afraid of retaliation, debt, or having nowhere to go, that’s not loyalty — that’s coercion.

America already has models for this: safe, confidential exit support (legal orientation, counseling, career transition). An AAA-style service for conscience would let people quit lawfully without destroying their lives.

You don’t change a culture by attacking it.
You change it by making ethical exit possible.

If we believe in responsibility, we should support a safe way out.

#signaliscorrupt

comment.base:
The problem isn’t one story or one platform—it’s a pattern. Large parts of the mainstream media have spent years framing events to inflame tensions and provoke outrage, normalizing conflict instead of reporting facts.
This narrative isn’t limited to traditional outlets; it’s amplified across social platforms where calls for action and confrontation are increasingly normalized. The effect is predictable: polarization grows, trust erodes, and instability becomes more likely.
Accountability doesn’t come from shouting names—it comes from recognizing these patterns and refusing to let them go unchallenged. Observing and exposing behavior, not labels, is the only way to hold the media responsible.

comment.base: Reading the comments, many did not see the entire video and what you did see, you interpreted to fit your narrative. Ms. Gould was in fact using her vehicle to block ICE on the street. ICE tried to detain her because obstruction of ICE in performing their duties is a crime. She defied a lawful order and tried to escape. The agent at the front of the vehicle attempting to block is the one who shot her as she hit him with her vehicle. Ms. Gould should not have tried to Obstruct ICE agents, had she complied when they tried to detain her, she’d be alive today. This is a tragic outcome no doubt but, Ms. Gould is a victim to her own poor choices.

comment.base:
If you want to stick it to the surveillance state, or even just ensure you can always keep in contact with your friends and family, download and encourage other people to download bitchat. This app has already shown its usefulness in Madagascar and nepal. Just by becoming part of the bluetooth mesh, you will be helping to create an entirely separate private communication network. Enough people doing so will make it so the system keeps working as long as people have their phones on, even in areas without internet or cell service. Like after a natural disaster, so every download isn't just a rejection of surveillance. It's an investment in you, your families, and your communities safety that will always be there for you even if you don't use it every day.
It would also help anyone who needs to stay under the radar by enabling burner phones to work without needing tracable signals the more people who have it.
